explanation.text: Jem explains that low gravity left her bones too thin and her muscles too weak to survive on Earth. Therefore, being “sentenced to life in space” means she must remain on the station to stay healthy.
The shuttle cannot see to dock, and lowering its shields would endanger everyone aboard. That crisis fills the command room with worry, so “tension” means worry in this sentence.
Jem's father immediately asks whether she is in touch with the shuttle and rushes her to the S.A.T. booth. His urgent question shows that he is surprised she can communicate with it.
Jem reveals that the old S.A.T. still works and gives a technician her code. Because she shows the crew how to contact the shuttle, they can send the docking coordinates in paragraph 28.
The statement that Jem was born on Space Station Alpha directly names the place where the story occurs. The other quotations describe actions but do not identify the setting.
Jem's eleven years aboard the station gave her knowledge of its forgotten S.A.T. system. Calling those years an advantage supports the theme that an unusual experience can become a valuable strength.
The programs described just before this word are designed to save snow leopards while helping herders. Since their purpose is to protect the cats, “conservation” means protection.
Paragraph 6 says researchers need more reliable leopard counts. Paragraph 9 then describes motion-and-heat-sensitive camera traps, therefore providing one method for obtaining those counts.
Paragraph 6 says scientists cannot judge whether conservation is working without reliable leopard counts. That need explains why McCarthy's team traveled to Kyrgyzstan to test counting methods.
The estimate of only 3,500 to 7,500 surviving snow leopards shows how threatened the species is. This evidence supports the article's main focus on why the cats need protection.

The article repeatedly links causes to results: habitat loss reduces prey, hungry leopards attack livestock, and herders then kill them. It also explains how protection programs can interrupt that chain.
The table gathers threats such as illegal hunting, habitat and prey loss, killings by herders, and weak protection. These causes show why snow leopards are struggling to survive.
People compare Kanikiya's graceful dancing to moonlight on a running river. Their admiration shows that others recognize her special talent, an idea that matters throughout the folktale.
Kanikiya believes the brolgas' mournful dance shows that they understand her sorrow. Feeling understood warms her emotionally, so the phrase suggests comfort rather than anger or wisdom.
After Kanikiya is kept from dancing, she cannot eat or smile and feels a growing heaviness in her heart. Those details show that the “mournful” days are sad ones.
Kanikiya's decision to slip away and dance with the cranes causes her disappearance and transformation. Because that action creates the story's climax, it most clearly develops the plot.
Being confined to camp makes Kanikiya weak and miserable, while dancing with the cranes promises peace. By the end, she leaves the camp and becomes a crane so she can live in happiness.

Kanikiya's feeling that she must dance drives the central conflict with her mother and ultimately leads her to join the cranes. Therefore, that motivation is essential to a summary of the whole story.
